writings.title: Bring What You Have and Watch God Multiply
writings.scriptureText: You give them something to eat, he responded.
writings.content:
This is Jesus's reply to the disciples when they mention that the crowd who had been listening to him needed food. The disciples' suggestion was to send the crowd away, so that the crowd could feed themselves. Jesus, probably knowing what miracle He was about to perform, first instructs the disciples to give the crowd something to eat. Why? Could Jesus not have just performed the miracle right then and there? Certainly He could have. However, if Jesus had performed the miracle on the spot, the same lesson would not have been taught to the disciples. Here is what I believe was going on in Jesus's head to respond in this way:

**1. Jesus wanted to emphasize that we *cannot* do this life on our own.** Sometimes, we need to stop and realize how little we ourselves really have to provide. All that we have comes from God. When Jesus responded in this way, let's take a look at the disciples' response:

> "They said to Him, 'Should we go and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread and give them something to eat?'" — Mark 6:37

While it is obviously hard to tell the tone of how the disciples replied, I believed that this was a reply trying to get Jesus to realize how *absurd* His response was. A denarii was one day's wage. The disciples are thus saying, you really think we can go and afford food worth 200 *days* of work for the people? Come on man, that's *absurd.* Jesus was teaching the disciples that what is impossible for man is possible for God (Matthew 19:16). What we cannot provide, God can.

**2. Once we realize how absurd/desperate the situation really is, and that we ourselves cannot deal with it, Jesus then teaches us to look at what we have.** After the disciples pointed out how absurd it would be for them to provide food for everyone, Jesus responds in this way:

> "He asked them, 'How many loaves do you have? Go and see.'" — Mark 6:38

We need to remember what we are going into the situation with. Jesus asks the disciples how many loaves they had so that they could see what He was about to do. If we fail to recognize what we have before we come to God, we will have no idea what He has done for us after He has provided. Jesus was teaching the disciples that we need to know and remember where we were before we come to God with a problem, so that we can know and appreciate where God takes us.

I believe the disciples also did not learn this lesson, at least not right away:

> "Then He got into the boat with them, and the wind ceased. They were completely astounded, because they had not understood about the loaves. Instead, their hearts were hardened." — Mark 6:51-52

> "Aware of this, He said to them, 'Why are you discussing the fact you have no bread? Don't you understand or comprehend? Do you have hardened hearts? Do you have eyes and not see; do you have ears and not hear? And do you not remember?'" — Mark 8:17-18

Even having witnessed this miracle first-hand, it was still in the disciples' nature to question Jesus and God. To rely on their own understanding and not God's. To look to themselves and not to Christ. Our hearts are easily hardened — we need to remember to allow God to soften them. Like the disciples, we see God do the miraculous, and yet we will still doubt. We all need to remember to ask God to not let our hearts harden to His wondrous testimonies.

**3. The last teaching that I see Jesus have here is God can take what we have and multiply it for His purposes.** This one is clearly seen in the story. The people have five loaves of bread and two fish. They bring them to Jesus, who brings it to God, who then provides enough to satisfy everyone's hunger and have more remaining (twelve baskets full of bread and fish). Let us not forget that it is God who multiplies.

Another beautiful thing about this part of the story is how God uses the offering of the *few* to satisfy the *many*. The disciples went around to all the people and found five loaves of bread and two fish. People offered what they had — while it was enough for themselves and their family, it was not enough for the entire crowd. Instead of being greedy and hoarding it for themselves though, they were willing to give it up to share with others. And what did they lose? Absolutely nothing. Instead, it was only gain — there was more bread at the end than the beginning and *everyone* was satisfied. These people offered what they had and God did not let them down. When we give God all that we have, even though it might be practically nothing (especially for the situation), we will see Him take care of others *and* ourselves. We can trust Him. We just have to be willing to give him all of our bread.

writings.title: Simply Thankful
writings.content:
A beautiful verse. So simple and plain, but one that we often forget to practice. We come to God with our wants and our needs, but do we come with our gratitude? Hardly, if ever! Why is it so hard? All we do is want. It is when the gospel is least on our minds that our wants and desires are at their most high. I am not saying that want is necessarily a bad thing — God has given us desires that are good to pursue. We should *want* to want to spend time with God. That is a good desire. To be happy is a good desire, so long as we are happy and content with and in God. But oh how we can fall into the illusion that we need this, that, or the other thing to be happy. Rarely, do I stop and think about what God has done for me. Yes, I can thank God for certain things in my life daily: my wife mainly and now our little daughter, the food that I eat, that I'm alive and woke up. But is my heart truly grateful to God or am I just doing these things out of dead religion? I think it is hard to have a heart that is genuinely grateful to God, when we are just thanking Him for the same things over and over. That is not to say that I should not thank God for these things daily, but when that is all I thank Him for, I find the gratitude is not there as it *ought* to be. I have been given far too much to not be grateful. I have been given the precious blood of Jesus Christ. With this alone, I should be the most grateful human being ever. All the rest of God's blessings will be seen as just that — *blessings*. The truth of the matter is this — I have lived a life worthy of damnation, not heaven. We all have: > "For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." — Romans 3:23 > "The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." — Romans 6:23 It is easy to despair when reading Romans 3:23. We have fallen short of the glory of God, the Creator of heaven and earth. He created us with intention, purpose, and worth and we have completely degraded it in ourselves and others. We have taken this valuable gift of life and told God that we know how to live it better than He could ever have designed us. Today, I listened to a sermon by C.H. Spurgeon titled, *"In Him: Like Him"*, where he said: > "Sin promised freedom, but brought us bondage." — C.H. Spurgeon Our sin promised us freedom — to live a life unconstrained from the design of God. Surely we should do what deep down we feel we want to do. If it goes against the nature of God, so be it. I want to do it and so I shall. I know better than God. And our reward? Bondage — we will become so enslaved to our sin we will find that we cannot get out of it. The wages of our sin is death. But wait, we have been given a gift! In Christ Jesus our Lord — eternal life! How can we as Christians not be *the most grateful people?* We take our eyes off of the cross and put the focus back on ourselves. It is necessary to die to ourselves and our own will. And painful. Oh so painful is the process of sanctification. For once we have been born again in Christ, we are at war. There is a war continually going on inside us between the old self and the new self. We know the ways of God, but we fall short time and time again on our own pursuit of holiness. The Apostle Paul said it best: > "For I do not do the good that I want to do, but I practice the evil that I do not want to do… So I discover this law: When I want to do what is good, evil is present with me. For in my inner self I delight in God's law, but I see a different law in the parts of my body, waging war against the law of my mind and taking me prisoner to the law of sin in the parts of my body. What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death? Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, with my mind I myself am serving the law of God, but with my flesh, the law of sin." — Romans 7:19, 21-25 Paul found a new kind of law, such that when he tried to do good, sin sprang up in him. The good he *wanted* to do, he didn't do. But the evil he *did not want* to do he kept on doing. As Spurgeon said, sin promised freedom but actually only brought about bondage. When we give our lives to Christ we are changed. No longer do we want to practice our evil ways but follow the good ways of the LORD. But we cannot do this without God. He will rescue us through Christ Jesus. He will sanctify us. He has given us eternal life. And for this, we should be thankful! When we remember our old condition, the bondage we lived in to sin, and the rescue that Jesus Christ brought about, we should have all the gratitude we need. The other blessings God brings about in our lives: family, food, shelter, friends, etc. will become all the more sweet. More importantly, we will find that our wants change — both in what we want to pursue, and the abundance of things we want. Godliness with contentment is great gain (1 Timothy 6:6) — such that we will want godliness and be content with it. I encourage you all to remember the gratitude we should have to God through the death and resurrection of Christ for our sins. Give God the glory and give Him this gratitude — the gratitude He deserves. And be thankful. For the little things, the big things, and all the things in between.
For when I see what He has done for me,
How could my heart not sing with glee?

Wretched man that I was and still am,
God has saved me with His unblemished lamb.

Now I can come before Him with thanksgiving,
And eternal life shall I start living.

writings.title: His Comfort for Our Joy
writings.scriptureText: When I am filled with cares, Your comfort brings me joy.
writings.content:
What a beautiful verse that we should always have at the forefront of our minds. We are filled with cares and God’s comfort brings us joy. But what cares was David talking about in this Psalm? The Hebrew word that cares is translated from is śar‘appîm (שַׂרְעַפִּים) which refers to disquieting, anxious, or divided thoughts that disturb the mind, often reflecting a multitude of internal worries. So, from this verse, our cares are those thoughts that disturb our mind. If we look further into the verse, we see that it has the pattern: when I, then God. When *I* am filled with cares then *God’s* comforts bring me joy. What are we looking to when our thoughts are divided and our mind is disturbed? Do we look at ourselves, the world, or anything that is not God to try to be comforted? I would assume for all of us, the answer is yes (at least a lot more of the time than it should be). Well, what are God’s comforts? Or, how does God’s comfort bring joy? God’s comfort lies in His word and in His character. When we are filled with cares, I challenge you to spend time meditating on God’s character and on His true Word. What care are you filled with today? Perhaps you are worried about your soul\! The enemy throws thought after thought after thought at you about your past. Remember God’s word: >“But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.” *\- 1 Corinthians 6:11* This verse was written by the Apostle Paul, but it is exactly what is needed when the enemy tempts us to doubt our salvation due to the past. It even seems in this case that Paul might be admonishing the Corinthians for practicing some unacceptable behavior after having been washed, sanctified, and justified. Sometimes, our thoughts get the best of us and they cause us to act in a way that is unacceptable for a Christian. We need to remind ourselves that we were washed, sanctified, and justified in Jesus’s name and by the Holy Spirit. Perhaps you say, “this is a good verse but it is not God’s word.” This is not entirely true. However, for the sake of argument, let us look to what the LORD said Himself for this same care I mentioned earlier: >“Do not fear, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by your name; you are mine.” \- Isaiah 43:1 Different verse, similar context. Do not fear your salvation. God has redeemed you. In fact, He called you *specifically* and you are His. When we really think, reflect, and meditate on this verse, how can we not find joy in the comfort of our God and what He has done for us? Perhaps you would argue further that these promises of old cannot be for you. Well, I can assure you that if you are in Christ this promise is for you: >“For every one of God’s promises is ‘Yes’ in him \[Jesus\].” \- 2 Corinthians 1:20 Notice that this does not say *some* of God’s promises but *every one* of God’s promises is “yes” in Jesus. This is wonderful\! Pick any time in the Bible that you see God make a promise. This promise has then been given to you through Jesus Christ. And this is the reason we *must* have much of God’s promises and truth built up within us. For, if we do not have some of God’s promises known, how will we combat our cares? I provided examples for only one scenario, however, we need to build up our arsenal of God’s truth to combat our cares. This is seen perfectly with the armor of God: >“Finally, be strengthened by the Lord and by his vast strength. Put on the full armor of God so that you can stand against the schemes of the devil. For our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers of this darkness, against evil, spiritual forces in the heavens. For this reason take up the full armor of God, so that you may be able to resist in the evil day, and having prepared everything, to take your stand. Stand, therefore, with truth like a belt around your waist, righteousness like armor on your chest, and your feet sandaled with readiness for the gospel of peace. In every situation take up the shield of faith with which you can extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one. Take the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit—which is the word of God.” \- Ephesians 6:10-17 Why do we need the armor of God? So that we can stand against the schemes of the devil\! Notice that Paul does not tell us to put on *some* of the armor of God but the *full* armor of God. If we want to stand a chance we need truth, righteousness, the gospel of peace, faith, salvation, and the word of God. We *cannot* walk in this life expecting victory without these things. In my view, many of our cares are planted by the enemy. Especially doubts \- the devil will use whatever tricks he can to make you doubt your faith in God. We must stand firm then with the full armor of God. For with the full armor of God equipped, our cares will be replaced with joy. We turn away from ourselves and toward God whose faithful love endures forever (Psalm 136). I promise you (and I myself need to remember this promise more than most\!) that if we know, meditate, and reflect on God’s character, our cares will be turned into joy by Him.
The devil schemes
In my head with hateful screams

Such that I am filled with anxious care
Then I remember that you know my every hair

So I suit up everyday
And know the LORD will take my cares away

writings.title: A Time to Sow
writings.scriptureText: Sow righteousness for yourselves and reap faithful love; break up your unplowed ground. It is time to seek the LORD until He comes and sends righteousness on you like the rain.
writings.content:
We are to sow, so that we may reap. In our modern age, we get anything and everything right when we want it. Need some food? Order it on doordash. Want some clothes? Get them the next day with Amazon. Therefore, we have become disconnected with sowing and reaping spiritually. We are far too used to getting what we want when we want it. When we come before God and ask for the desires of our heart, we expect to get them right away. Even if they are good desires, God has a timing that is better than our own. We must learn to take time to sow, so that we may reap. There is a promise here though, *if you sow* righteousness *you will reap* faithful love. To do so, we need to break up our unplowed ground. Therefore, in order to sow seed for ourselves, we must first be plowed. This process may be painful, but it is necessary. We must be prepared to let go of what we must in order to humbly receive the Word of God. Sometimes, in the process, our sinful nature will be revealed as so much more sinful than we ever thought. And that is the point. We need to know how deprived of righteousness we are on our own, so that we can turn to the only one who can save us, Jesus Christ. How do we sow this righteousness for ourselves? We must seek the LORD so that He will come and send righteousness on us like the rain. The righteousness is poured onto us *from* Him. Let us not be deceived into believing that this righteousness is our own: >“All of us have become like something unclean, and all our righteous acts are like a polluted garment; all of us wither like a leaf, and our iniquities carry us away like the wind.” \- Isaiah 64:6 A rather humbling verse to read. All of our righteous acts are a polluted garment. When we strive for righteousness on our own, the corruption of our sin will pollute the act in some way. How can this be? Well, an example could be that we desire to do something good in hopes that this will in turn benefit ourselves more. Therefore, the good act is stained by our own selfish ambition. I’m sure there are many other examples that we could think of. The point is, when we strive on our own, our sinful nature will always trip us up. Therefore, we *must* seek the LORD and He *will* come and send righteousness on us. For remember the LORD and the Lord Jesus Christ, respectively said: >“You will seek me and find me when you search for me with all your heart.” \- Jeremiah 29:13 >“Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will be opened to you. For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened.” \- Matthew 7:7-8 These are two very important promises that we receive from the Father and the Son that should inspire us in our pursuit of sowing righteousness. Seek God with your whole heart and He will be found. For when we seek we will indeed find. What is important is that we need our whole heart dedicated to finding the LORD. We cannot be half-hearted in this. I see this in my own life, I want to seek the LORD with all that I am, but I often let various distractions grab my attention. Before I know it, the day is up and I am no closer to seeing God than I was when the day started. In fact, I may be further. I become frustrated that I don’t see God as I would have liked, but no wonder, when I seek with half my heart. C.S. Lewis said it best: >“It would seem that Our Lord finds our desires not too strong, but too weak. We are half-hearted creatures, fooling about with drink and sex and ambition when infinite joy is offered us, like an ignorant child who wants to go on making mud pies in a slum because he cannot imagine what is meant by the offer of a holiday at the sea. We are far too easily pleased.” \- C.S. Lewis (The Weight of Glory) This is true in my life. Infinite joy is offered to me and yet I would rather waste away with some worldly pleasure that leaves me broken and unsatisfied. I seek to be filled up with God’s creation and not by God Himself. I used to think that this meant my desires and passions (lust, greed, idolatry, etc.) were too strong, and they needed to be quenched with a lesser desire. But, I now find that my desires were actually too weak. My desire to be happy and whole was and is too weak, for if I wanted it truly, I would find it in the only true source, God Himself. I need to remember: >“In your presence is abundant joy; at your right hand are eternal pleasures.” \- Psalm 16:11 It is time to seek the LORD \- in His presence my joy will be abundant. This joy is not 99% joy, but greater than 100%. That is abundance – more than we can take. Additionally, my longing for pleasure will be satisfied as well in the presence of God with eternal pleasures. Things that I cannot even imagine. Oh, my soul, it is time to seek\! As I close and reflect, the main verse is a verse I have meditated on a bit, but as I return to it today, I realize that I always thought that sowing righteousness meant striving to do good acts. However, when I look back on Isaiah 64:6, I no longer think this is true. Sowing righteousness is about seeking the LORD. Seek Him through his word every day. Seek Him in His creation, remembering that He made the earth and all of its inhabitants. Seek union with Him through His Son. When we seek God, we will find Him. And as we come to know God, and sow His word, truth, and promises into ourselves, the Holy Spirit will pour righteousness into our lives. We will become like Christ (1 John 3:2). So come, let us seek the LORD with our *whole* heart and not waver in our pursuit, for we will find Him. And it will be glorious.
Oh my soul it is time to sow
For it is the LORD you wish to know

Let go of all you hold dear
So that to the LORD you may draw near

And when we think what we’ve lost will bring pain
We will see that with the LORD all is gain
